question: Article 9(6)-(8) of the EU AI Act mandates testing of High-Risk AI systems. What specific testing requirements must the AI Review Committee implement for the credit scoring AI before and during deployment?
ground_truth: Article 9(6)-(8) establishes mandatory testing requirements for High-Risk AI systems: (1) Purpose of testing (Art.9(6)) — testing must identify the most appropriate and targeted risk management measures and ensure High-Risk AI performs consistently for its intended purpose and complies with Chapter III Section 2 requirements; (2) Real-world condition testing option (Art.9(7)) — testing procedures may include testing in real-world conditions per Article 60; this applies when laboratory testing cannot adequately simulate actual deployment conditions; for credit scoring AI, controlled pilot testing on actual loan applications with heightened human oversight may be appropriate; (3) Pre-market testing obligation (Art.9(8)) — testing must be performed as appropriate throughout the development process AND in any event prior to placing on the market or putting into service; testing cannot be deferred to post-deployment; the 'in any event' language makes pre-deployment testing mandatory without exception; (4) Metrics and thresholds requirement (Art.9(8)) — testing must be carried out against prior defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds appropriate to the intended purpose; for credit scoring AI, the committee must pre-define: AUC/Gini target thresholds; maximum acceptable false rejection rate; fairness metric thresholds (adverse impact ratio minimums); minimum explanation quality scores; (5) Vulnerable population testing (Art.9(9)) — testing must specifically assess impacts on persons under 18 and other vulnerable groups; (6) Testing documentation — all testing procedures, metrics, thresholds, and results must be documented in the technical documentation (Annex IV); (7) AI Review Committee obligation — require submission of pre-defined testing thresholds and test results before granting deployment approval; reject submissions that did not test against pre-defined thresholds.
notes: Art.9 테스트 의무; 사전 정의 임계값 기반 테스트

question: The bank wants to test the credit scoring AI in real-world conditions with actual loan applicants before full deployment. What safeguards does the EU AI Act require under Article 60?
ground_truth: Article 60 provides a framework for testing High-Risk AI in real-world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes. For credit scoring AI real-world testing: (1) Provider responsibility (Art.60(1)) — the provider (bank if internally developed) bears responsibility for real-world testing; testing must be conducted in accordance with a testing plan; (2) Testing plan requirements (Art.60(2)) — the plan must cover: the AI system description; testing objectives and conditions; metrics for assessing the AI system; testing duration and location; protection measures for subjects; (3) Fundamental rights safeguards (Art.60(2)(g)) — testing must not lead to 'excessive intrusion into the private sphere' or other fundamental rights violations; for credit testing, this means: applicants must not receive inferior credit terms solely due to their participation in testing; testing cannot disadvantage vulnerable groups; testing must be limited to what is necessary for assessment; (4) Informed consent (Art.61) — persons subject to real-world AI testing must be informed: that an AI system is being tested; the purpose and expected duration; their rights including right to withdraw; that participation is voluntary and refusal has no adverse consequences; for credit applicants in real-world testing: they must consent before being subject to AI-driven credit assessment in the test phase; (5) Data protection — real-world testing data is subject to GDPR; special care regarding sensitive personal financial data; (6) Market surveillance authority notification — providers must notify the relevant national competent authority before commencing real-world testing; (7) AI Review Committee role — approve the testing plan before commencement; verify informed consent procedures are implemented; review testing results before full deployment approval.
notes: Art.60 실세계 조건 테스트; 적절한 안전장치 필요

question: The insurance subsidiary receives a life insurance pricing AI from a vendor (provider). The AI Review Committee examines the vendor's Article 13 instructions for use. What specific performance information must the instructions contain about the AI's accuracy across different population groups?
ground_truth: Article 13(3) specifies detailed content requirements for High-Risk AI instructions for use. Regarding performance across population groups: (1) Article 13(3)(b)(ii) — instructions must include 'the level of accuracy, including its metrics, and the relevant accuracy metrics including the accuracy achieved, as the case may be, in relation to specific persons or groups of persons on which the system is intended to be used'; for life insurance pricing AI this means: overall model accuracy metrics (e.g., loss ratio prediction accuracy, claims prediction AUC); performance disaggregated by: age groups (critically important for life insurance); health status categories; gender where relevant and legally permissible; geographic regions; income brackets; (2) Article 13(3)(b)(iv) — 'the performance of the High-Risk AI system in relation to the persons or groups of persons on which the system is intended to be used'; this goes further than just accuracy — it requires explanation of how the AI performs differently for different groups; (3) Article 13(3)(b)(iii) — 'known or foreseeable circumstances, related to the use of the High-Risk AI system in accordance with its intended purpose or under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse, which may lead to risks to the health and safety or fundamental rights referred to in Article 9(2)'; vendor must disclose known population groups for whom the model is less reliable or potentially biased; (4) Practical implication for AI Review Committee — reject vendor-provided instructions that provide only aggregate performance metrics without demographic disaggregation; require vendors to demonstrate testing across all relevant demographic segments; (5) Vendor contract clause — include a contractual requirement for vendors to update Art.13 instructions whenever performance data reveals new demographic disparities.
notes: Art.13(3) 집단별 성능 정보; 보험 AI 인구통계 성능 공개

question: The bank (as deployer) discovers that its vendor-provided credit scoring AI is producing significantly discriminatory outcomes for a specific ethnic minority group in France. What notification obligations arise under Article 26(5), and what is the timeline?
ground_truth: Article 26(5) creates an immediate notification obligation when deployers identify previously unknown risks: 'Where the deployer identifies a risk within the meaning of Article 9(2) and that risk was not previously identified by the provider, it shall, without undue delay, inform the provider of the AI system as well as the relevant market surveillance authority.' For discriminatory credit scoring outcomes: (1) Triggering condition — the discrimination constitutes a risk to fundamental rights (equal treatment, non-discrimination) within the meaning of Art.9(2)(a); this was presumably not previously identified by the vendor-provider (otherwise it should have been in the Art.13 instructions for use); Art.26(5) notification is therefore triggered; (2) Immediate action sequence: Step 1 — notify the vendor-provider 'without undue delay' (no specific timeframe; immediately upon confirmation of the discriminatory pattern); Step 2 — notify the relevant market surveillance authority in France 'without undue delay'; Step 3 — suspend use of the AI for affected customer segment pending investigation (Art.26(4) monitoring and suspension obligation); (3) What to report to the authority: identity of provider and deployer; description of the AI system and its function; nature of the identified risk (discriminatory outcomes for specific ethnic group); scope (number of affected persons, time period, geographic area); immediate actions taken (suspension, notification to provider); (4) Connection to Art.73 serious incident reporting — if the discrimination constitutes a serious incident (violation of EU law protecting fundamental rights at scale), Art.73 serious incident reporting may also apply; (5) Regulatory consequence — market surveillance authorities will investigate; the vendor-provider faces potential enforcement action; the bank may also face regulatory scrutiny for its deployment monitoring practices.
notes: Art.26(5) 리스크 통보; 차별 발견 시 즉각 조치

question: Article 9(9) of the EU AI Act requires consideration of adverse impacts on persons under 18 and other vulnerable groups. How should the AI Review Committee implement this requirement for the credit scoring AI?
ground_truth: Article 9(9) requires that when implementing the risk management system, providers give consideration to whether the High-Risk AI system is likely to have an adverse impact on persons under the age of 18 and, as appropriate, other vulnerable groups. For credit scoring AI: (1) Persons under 18 — credit scoring AI used for lending to minors is uncommon (most lending requires legal capacity); however: if the AI processes guarantor information or family financial data that may include minors; if the AI is used for student loan products with minor co-signers; the committee should assess these edge cases; (2) Vulnerable groups beyond minors — 'other vulnerable groups' is not exhaustively defined but clearly includes: elderly persons with potential cognitive decline or digital literacy limitations; persons with disabilities (physical or cognitive) that may affect their financial data patterns; financially distressed persons who may be particularly harmed by incorrect AI credit decisions; persons with limited financial literacy; persons from communities historically underserved by formal financial systems; (3) Assessment methodology — for each vulnerable group: identify how the AI system may disproportionately affect them; assess whether training data adequately represents them; test model performance specifically for these groups; evaluate whether error rates are disproportionately higher; (4) Mitigation measures for vulnerable groups: mandatory human review for credit decisions affecting identified vulnerable groups; lower AI-driven rejection thresholds triggering automatic human escalation; enhanced explanation quality standards for adverse decisions affecting vulnerable groups; (5) Documentation — Art.9(9) considerations must be documented in the Art.9 risk management system and referenced in technical documentation; (6) AI Review Committee gate — require explicit vulnerable group impact analysis as a pre-deployment submission requirement.
notes: Art.9(9) 취약계층 영향 평가; 미성년자 및 기타 취약 그룹

question: Article 18 of the EU AI Act requires providers to keep technical documentation for a defined period after the AI system is placed on the market. What are the retention requirements and how should the AI Review Committee manage document lifecycle?
ground_truth: Article 18 establishes documentation retention obligations for High-Risk AI providers: (1) Core retention obligation (Art.18(1)) — providers must keep technical documentation, logs (where within their control), and the EU Declaration of Conformity for a period of 10 years after the High-Risk AI system has been placed on the EU market or put into service; (2) Serious incident documentation — documentation related to serious incidents reported under Art.73 must be retained and available for authority review; (3) Relevance for the bank: as provider (internally developed AI) — the 10-year retention clock starts when the AI system is first deployed in the EU; as deployer (vendor AI) — the vendor-provider has the 10-year retention obligation; the bank should contractually require vendors to maintain documentation for the required period and confirm continued access; (4) What must be retained: complete technical documentation per Annex IV (at all versions); EU Declaration of Conformity; logs of AI system operation to the extent within the provider's control; records of the quality management system (Art.17); records of conformity assessment procedures; any serious incident reports; (5) Version control — technical documentation must be kept up to date (Art.11(1)); historical versions must also be retained to enable reconstruction of past AI system states for legal and regulatory purposes; (6) Post-decommissioning obligations — the 10-year retention period runs from market placement, not decommissioning; documentation obligations survive AI system retirement; (7) AI Review Committee obligation — establish a document lifecycle management system ensuring 10-year retention for all High-Risk AI technical documentation from first EU deployment date.
notes: Art.18 문서 보존 10년; 퇴역 후에도 지속

question: Article 21 of the EU AI Act requires High-Risk AI providers to cooperate with national competent authorities on request. What does this obligation entail for a Korean bank operating in the EU, and how should the AI Review Committee prepare?
ground_truth: Article 21 requires providers of High-Risk AI to cooperate with national competent authorities in performing their tasks. For a Korean bank with EU operations: (1) Scope of cooperation obligation (Art.21(1)) — upon a reasoned request, providers must take all necessary steps to: provide competent authorities with access to technical documentation and information necessary to assess AI system compliance; enable market surveillance authorities to inspect AI systems; provide information about the AI system's capabilities and limitations; (2) Cooperation with test access — upon request, providers must allow market surveillance authorities access to the AI system itself for assessment purposes; this may include providing access to model APIs or controlled test environments; (3) National contact point — providers established outside the EU must designate an authorised representative (Art.22) who serves as the point of contact for competent authority cooperation; the Korean bank's EU authorised representative handles cooperation requests; (4) Timeliness — cooperation must be prompt; 'necessary steps' implies active engagement rather than passive response; (5) Confidentiality protection — information provided under Art.21 is subject to Art.78 confidentiality obligations; competent authorities cannot disclose commercially sensitive information to third parties; (6) Preparation for the AI Review Committee: maintain examination-ready documentation packages for all High-Risk AI systems; designate a named regulatory liaison for EU AI Act authority interactions; establish a protocol for responding to authority requests (triage → legal review → response within target timeline); train staff on cooperation obligations; (7) Financial regulator relationship — in many EU jurisdictions, the financial services regulator (e.g., BaFin, ACPR) may be designated as the competent authority for financial AI; leverage existing regulatory relationships.
notes: Art.21 당국 협력 의무; 한국 은행의 EU 당국 대응

question: Article 62 of the EU AI Act provides support measures for SMEs and startups. Can smaller financial institutions (e.g., credit unions, small fintech lenders) benefit from these measures?
ground_truth: Article 62 establishes measures to support SMEs including startups in EU Member States: (1) Priority access to regulatory sandboxes (Art.62(1)(a)) — SMEs and startups are given priority access to AI regulatory sandboxes established under Art.57; smaller financial institutions can use sandboxes to test novel credit AI or insurance AI under regulatory supervision before full compliance burden applies; (2) Separate communication channels (Art.62(1)(b)) — competent authorities must establish specific communication channels for SMEs and startups to raise questions and receive information on implementation; smaller financial institutions can use these channels to seek clarity on how EU AI Act applies to their specific AI systems; (3) Dedicated support (Art.62(1)(c)) — Member States may provide tailored guidance, simplified compliance documents, and templates specifically designed for SMEs; smaller financial institutions should actively seek these resources; (4) Reduced fees for SMEs (Art.62(2)) — for conformity assessment procedures (Art.43), Member States may establish reduced fees for microenterprises, small and medium-sized enterprises; (5) Definition of SME — under EU definition, SMEs are enterprises with fewer than 250 employees and either annual turnover ≤€50M or balance sheet ≤€43M; most credit unions and smaller fintech lenders qualify; (6) Practical guidance for smaller financial institutions: identify the EU Member State(s) competent authority offering SME support; request access to regulatory sandboxes for novel AI testing; use SME-simplified compliance templates where available; join industry associations providing collective compliance guidance; (7) Equal compliance obligations — SME support reduces compliance burden but does not reduce the substantive compliance obligations; High-Risk AI must still meet all Chapter III requirements.
notes: Art.62 중소기업 지원; 소규모 금융기관 EU AI Act 대응

question: Article 111 of the EU AI Act addresses AI systems already placed on the market before the Act's application. The bank has a credit scoring AI deployed in the EU before August 2026. What transitional provisions apply?
ground_truth: Article 111 establishes transitional provisions for AI systems already on the market before the EU AI Act's full application. For credit scoring AI deployed before August 2026: (1) General transitional rule (Art.111(1)) — High-Risk AI systems already placed on the EU market or put into service before the application date (August 2026) are exempt from Chapter III obligations IF they do not undergo a substantial modification after that date; this is a grandfather clause for pre-existing AI systems; (2) 'Substantial modification' significance — if the pre-existing credit scoring AI undergoes a substantial modification after August 2026: the transitional exemption is lost; the modified system must comply with all Chapter III obligations; this creates a strategic AI governance consideration: minor updates preserve the exemption; major redesigns or architectural changes trigger full compliance; (3) Voluntary compliance option — providers of pre-existing systems may voluntarily comply with all EU AI Act requirements before they are legally required; this is advisable as: regulatory examination of pre-existing systems is still possible; voluntary compliance demonstrates good faith; prepares the institution for the eventual compliance obligation when substantial modification occurs; (4) Time-limited exemption — the transitional exemption does not last indefinitely; the Commission reviews transitional provisions; as substantial modifications occur over time, compliance becomes progressively required; (5) AI Review Committee action plan for pre-existing AI: catalog all High-Risk AI systems deployed before August 2026; document their deployment dates to establish baseline; classify planned changes as substantial modification (full compliance required) or non-substantial (exemption preserved); develop a compliance roadmap for pre-existing systems as they undergo updates.
notes: Art.111 경과 조치; 기존 배포 AI 시스템 유예

question: Article 40 of the EU AI Act references harmonised standards for AI. What is the significance of harmonised standards for financial institutions' AI compliance, and what presumption does Article 42 create?
ground_truth: Articles 40 and 42 establish the role of harmonised standards in EU AI Act compliance: (1) Article 40 — Harmonised Standards: the European Commission can request standardisation bodies (CEN, CENELEC, ETSI) to develop harmonised standards for the EU AI Act; these standards will specify technical requirements for compliance with specific EU AI Act provisions; for financial AI, standards may address: AI risk management systems (aligned with ISO/IEC 23894); AI trustworthiness characteristics (aligned with ISO/IEC TR 24028); testing methodologies for credit scoring AI; fairness metrics and measurement methodologies; (2) Article 42 — Presumption of Conformity: 'High-risk AI systems that are in conformity with harmonised standards or parts thereof the references of which have been published in the Official Journal of the European Union shall be presumed to be in conformity with the requirements set out in Chapter III Section 2 of this Regulation'; this creates a legal safe harbor — compliance with harmonised standards presumptively satisfies EU AI Act requirements; (3) Practical benefit for financial institutions: standardised test methodologies provide clarity on how to demonstrate Art.9 testing compliance; standardised documentation formats simplify Art.11 technical documentation preparation; presumption of conformity reduces regulatory uncertainty and litigation risk; (4) Current standards landscape (as of 2026): ISO/IEC 42001 (AI Management Systems) is a candidate for harmonised standard status; ISO/IEC 23894 (AI Risk Management) aligns with Art.9; work is ongoing by CEN/CENELEC JTC 21; (5) AI Review Committee action — adopt available ISO/IEC AI standards now; monitor Official Journal for harmonised standard publications; update governance framework when standards are published to benefit from presumption of conformity.
notes: Art.40/42 조화 표준; 적합 추정 법적 효과

question: The AI Review Committee is designing a standardized technical documentation template for all High-Risk AI systems. What must Annex IV documentation include for the credit scoring AI specifically?
ground_truth: Annex IV specifies the minimum content for technical documentation of High-Risk AI systems. For credit scoring AI, the standardized template must include: (1) General description (Annex IV §1): intended purpose (creditworthiness evaluation for personal loans up to X amount); version information; how it interacts with other hardware/software; how the AI system is made available (licensed software, API, embedded in loan origination system); third-party tools and components used; (2) Detailed description of elements and development process (Annex IV §2): the logic of the AI: the overall model architecture (gradient boosting, neural network, etc.); training methodology including data preparation, feature engineering, model selection; training datasets with description of source, characteristics, coverage (geographic, demographic, temporal), and quality assurance measures; (3) Validation and testing (Annex IV §3): results of accuracy and robustness measurements; test sets used (description and characteristics); test methodology; performance metrics including disaggregated performance by demographic group; testing against pre-defined thresholds per Art.9(8); fairness testing results; (4) Monitoring and control (Annex IV §4): post-market monitoring plan per Art.72; logging capabilities per Art.12; (5) Risk management (Annex IV §5): summary of the Art.9 risk management system; identified risks and mitigation measures; residual risk assessment; (6) Changes made to the system throughout its lifecycle (Annex IV §6): change log with description of changes and assessment of whether they constitute substantial modifications; (7) Standards applied (Annex IV §7): list of harmonized standards applied or reference to common specifications; (8) EU Declaration of Conformity (Annex IV §8): copy of the Art.47 declaration; (9) Post-market monitoring (Annex IV §9): description of the Art.72 system.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Article 11; Annex IV
notes: Annex IV 기술 문서 표준 템플릿; 신용 AI 특화

question: The bank uses generative AI to create personalized investment summaries and financial health reports for customers. Article 50(2) requires marking of AI-generated content. How must this marking be implemented?
ground_truth: Article 50(2) requires providers of AI systems that generate synthetic audio, image, video, or text content to ensure the outputs are marked in a machine-readable format and detectable as artificially generated. For AI-generated investment summaries and financial health reports: (1) Marking obligation scope — Art.50(2) covers AI systems generating 'synthetic audio, image, video or text content'; personalized investment summaries and financial health reports clearly fall within 'text content'; (2) Machine-readable format requirement — the marking must be machine-readable (not just visible to humans); this enables detection by automated tools; practical implementation: embed watermarking or metadata in generated text documents; use standardized content provenance frameworks (C2PA — Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity); include AI-generation metadata in document headers; (3) Human-readable disclosure — while Art.50(2) focuses on machine-readable marking, the AI Review Committee should also implement human-readable disclosure (e.g., 'This report was generated by AI' footer or header) to satisfy the spirit of transparency; (4) Detection capability — the marking must be detectable 'as artificially generated'; the bank should verify that its marking implementation can be detected by standard content provenance verification tools; (5) Exception for authorized use — Art.50(2) does not apply when AI-generated content is part of an evidently artistic, creative, satirical, fictional or analogous work; financial reports do not qualify for this exception; (6) Interaction with Art.50(1) — if the AI-generated document is delivered through an interactive AI system, Art.50(1) disclosure of AI interaction also applies; (7) Regulatory timeline — Art.50 was applicable from August 2026; all AI-generated financial documents distributed in the EU must be marked.
notes: Art.50(2) AI 생성 콘텐츠 표시; 기계 판독 가능 형식

question: Article 26(3) requires deployers to ensure that input data used in High-Risk AI is relevant and sufficiently representative. What obligations does this create for the bank using a vendor credit scoring AI with its own customer data as inputs?
ground_truth: Article 26(3) states: 'Where deployers can choose the input data, deployers shall ensure that those input data are relevant and sufficiently representative in view of the intended purpose of the High-Risk AI system.' For the bank as deployer feeding its customer data into a vendor credit scoring AI: (1) Data relevance obligation — the bank must ensure that the customer data it feeds into the vendor AI is relevant for the credit scoring purpose; inputs must have genuine predictive relevance to creditworthiness; data that introduces discrimination (e.g., using geographic data as proxy for ethnic origin) must be excluded; (2) Representativeness obligation — 'sufficiently representative' means the input data provided to the AI must represent the actual population being evaluated; if the bank selectively provides data in ways that distort the AI's performance (e.g., only providing data for certain customer segments), it violates Art.26(3); (3) Interaction with vendor AI design — if the vendor AI was designed and validated on a specific data schema, the bank must ensure its own data inputs conform to that schema; data format mismatches can degrade AI performance; (4) Data preprocessing responsibility — the bank controls data preprocessing before AI input; this creates responsibility for: handling missing values (imputation vs. exclusion); normalizing data ranges; handling outliers; ensuring data is current (not using stale financial data); (5) Documentation requirement — the bank should document its data preparation process and quality controls for inputs to the vendor AI; (6) Monitoring obligation connection — Art.26(4) requires monitoring of AI operation; this includes monitoring whether input data quality has degraded; (7) AI Review Committee role — require documentation of data preprocessing and quality control procedures as part of deployer approval for each High-Risk AI deployment.
notes: Art.26(3) 입력 데이터 배포자 의무; 데이터 품질 책임

question: Article 74 of the EU AI Act establishes market surveillance and control mechanisms. How might market surveillance activities affect a financial institution's AI operations, and how should the AI Review Committee prepare?
ground_truth: Article 74 grants market surveillance authorities broad powers to investigate and control AI systems in the EU market. For financial institutions: (1) Market surveillance authority powers (Art.74) — authorities may: access AI systems and related information; require testing; take samples; conduct investigations; order corrective actions including market withdrawal; impose temporary restrictions; (2) Specific financial AI surveillance — designated national competent authorities (which may be financial regulators in some Member States) will conduct targeted surveillance of High-Risk financial AI; credit scoring and insurance pricing AI are priority areas given their direct fundamental rights implications; (3) Investigation triggers — market surveillance may be triggered by: consumer complaints; serious incident reports per Art.73; whistleblower reports (Art.87); cross-border information sharing between authorities; proactive sector-wide investigations; (4) Cooperation during surveillance — Art.21 cooperation obligations apply during market surveillance; the bank must provide documentation, access, and testing support on request; (5) Remedies available to authorities (Art.79) — if surveillance reveals non-compliance: require corrective actions with timeline; order restriction or withdrawal from market; impose financial penalties per Art.99; (6) Proactive preparation for AI Review Committee: maintain examination-ready documentation at all times (technical documentation, QMS records, testing results); designate a named regulatory contact for EU AI Act market surveillance interactions; conduct annual internal compliance audits simulating market surveillance examination; understand which national authority(ies) have jurisdiction over the bank's AI systems in each EU Member State; (7) Multi-country coordination — for banks operating across multiple EU Member States, different national authorities may conduct parallel surveillance; the committee must coordinate responses.
notes: Art.74 시장 감시; 금융 AI 규제 검사 대비

question: The bank uses an LLM with systemic risk designation (above 10^25 FLOP training compute) for its AI credit explanation generator. How does Article 55 affect the bank's governance obligations?
ground_truth: Article 51 classifies GPAI models with systemic risk based on training compute exceeding 10^25 FLOPs or Commission designation. Article 55 establishes additional obligations for providers of such models. For the bank using a systemic risk GPAI model: (1) Who bears Article 55 obligations — the GPAI model provider (the AI company providing the LLM), not the bank (which is a downstream user); the bank's obligation is to select LLM providers who comply with Art.55; (2) Article 55 obligations for the LLM provider: adversarial testing (red-teaming) to identify systemic risks (Art.55(1)(a)); assessment and mitigation of systemic risks including their sources (Art.55(1)(b)); incident and corrective measure reporting to AI Office (Art.55(1)(c)); ensuring adequate cybersecurity protection (Art.55(1)(d)); (3) Impact on the bank's due diligence — when selecting or renewing a contract with a systemic risk LLM provider: require documentation of the provider's Art.55 compliance (adversarial testing results, risk mitigation measures); verify the provider has fulfilled reporting obligations to AI Office; assess whether the provider's risk mitigation measures adequately protect the bank's specific use case (credit explanation generation); (4) GPAI model documentation (Art.53) — all GPAI models (not just systemic risk) must provide downstream users with documentation; the bank should review the LLM provider's Art.53 documentation before deployment; (5) High-Risk AI system classification — if the credit explanation generator built on the systemic risk LLM is itself a High-Risk AI system (because it significantly influences credit decisions), the bank bears full Chapter III provider or deployer obligations for that system; (6) AI Review Committee — include LLM provider Art.55 compliance verification in vendor due diligence checklist.
notes: Art.55 시스템 리스크 GPAI; LLM 공급자 의무와 은행 실사

question: Article 87 of the EU AI Act protects persons reporting AI Act infringements. How does this whistleblowing framework affect the bank's AI governance culture, and what obligations does it create?
ground_truth: Article 87 establishes whistleblower protection for persons reporting EU AI Act violations: (1) Protected reporting (Art.87(1)) — any person who reports information on actual or potential infringements of the EU AI Act to competent authorities or the AI Office is protected; reporters include: employees of the AI provider/deployer; customers who identify AI-related harms; contractors or supply chain partners; (2) Protection scope (Art.87) — protected reporters must not face: dismissal or adverse employment consequences; retaliation or punishment; discrimination; (3) Reporting channels — the Act does not mandate specific internal reporting channels but encourages operators to establish them; external reporting to national competent authorities and AI Office is always available; (4) Confidentiality — competent authorities must protect the identity of reporters; (5) Implications for financial institutions' AI governance culture: the whistleblowing framework creates an external reporting mechanism that bypasses internal channels; employees who believe their institution is violating EU AI Act can report directly to national authorities; this incentivizes institutions to establish genuine, effective internal AI complaint mechanisms that employees trust; (6) Internal reporting channel design — the AI Review Committee should ensure internal AI concern reporting channels: are genuinely independent (not reporting to the AI development team or business line being reviewed); provide meaningful investigation and feedback; protect reporters from retaliation; are actively promoted to all staff; (7) Governance culture implication — institutions with strong internal AI governance cultures and safe reporting environments are less likely to face external whistleblower reports; the threat of external reporting motivates genuine internal compliance; (8) Connection to Art.99 penalties — external whistleblower reports may trigger market surveillance investigations and potential Art.99 penalties.
notes: Art.87 내부고발자 보호; AI 거버넌스 문화에 미치는 영향

question: The European AI Board established under Articles 65-66 will issue opinions on EU AI Act interpretation. How should the AI Review Committee monitor and incorporate Board opinions into its governance framework?
ground_truth: Articles 65 and 66 establish the European AI Board and its functions: (1) Board composition (Art.65) — composed of one representative from each Member State's national competent authority; Commission representative; EU AI Office may participate; (2) Board functions (Art.66) — the Board: advises and assists the Commission in implementation; collects and shares expertise; contributes to uniform application across Member States; issues opinions and recommendations; (3) Relevance for financial AI — the Board will issue opinions on: interpretation of 'High-Risk AI' classification criteria (affecting Annex III §5 financial AI); implementation guidance for Art.9 risk management; Art.10 data governance standards; human oversight models for specific High-Risk AI categories; (4) Legal status of Board opinions — Board opinions and recommendations do not have binding legal force but carry strong interpretive authority; national competent authorities are likely to follow Board guidance in enforcement decisions; (5) AI Review Committee monitoring obligations: subscribe to AI Office and Board publications and consultations; designate a regulatory intelligence function monitoring Board activities; participate in public consultations on Board opinions related to financial AI; (6) Implementation of Board opinions: when the Board issues guidance on financial AI High-Risk classification or compliance standards, the committee should assess current practices against the opinion and implement changes within a reasonable compliance timeline; (7) Industry engagement — financial sector associations (EBF, Insurance Europe, EFAMA) participate in Board advisory processes; the bank should monitor and contribute to these association positions; (8) Connection to Art.96 Commission guidelines — the Board feeds into Commission implementation guidelines (Art.96) that provide additional compliance clarity.
notes: 유럽 AI 이사회 기능; 금융 AI 해석 지침 모니터링

subcategory: Article 6 and Annex III §5(a) Essential Services
question: Annex III §5(a) classifies AI evaluating eligibility for essential public services as High-Risk. The bank administers government-backed mortgage guarantee programs using AI. Does this qualify?
ground_truth: Annex III §5(a) covers: 'AI systems intended to be used by public authorities or on behalf of public authorities to evaluate the eligibility of natural persons for essential public assistance benefits and services, including healthcare services, as well as to grant, reduce, revoke, or reclaim such benefits and services.' For bank administration of government-backed mortgage guarantee programs: (1) 'By or on behalf of public authorities' — this is the critical jurisdictional element; if the bank administers the guarantee program as an agent of a public authority (government), the AI may qualify under §5(a); if the bank independently uses AI for its own credit decisions on government-backed products, §5(a) may not apply (but §5(b) creditworthiness evaluation still applies); (2) 'Essential public assistance benefits and services' — government-backed mortgage guarantees are a form of public assistance enabling housing access; if the AI determines whether an applicant qualifies for the guarantee program (and thus for affordable mortgage access), this is evaluating eligibility for an essential public benefit; (3) Dual classification possible — a government-backed mortgage AI could potentially qualify under both §5(a) (if evaluating public guarantee eligibility as public authority agent) and §5(b) (creditworthiness evaluation); both classifications require the same Chapter III compliance; (4) Practical determination — the bank should review the contractual arrangements with the government authority: if the bank acts as government agent in administering the program, §5(a) applies; if the bank independently offers government-backed mortgages, §5(b) applies; (5) Same compliance obligations either way — whether §5(a) or §5(b) applies, the result is High-Risk AI with full Chapter III obligations; the classification question primarily affects which regulator has oversight.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Annex III §5(a); Article 6(2)
notes: Annex III §5(a) 필수 공공 서비스; 정부 보증 모기지 AI

question: Article 72(4) requires deployers to report relevant data to providers to support post-market monitoring. What data must the bank (as deployer) provide to its credit scoring AI vendor, and how should this data-sharing relationship be structured?
ground_truth: Article 72(4) states: 'Where deployers have concluded a service level agreement or another contractual arrangement with providers of High-Risk AI systems, the relevant data required for the post-market monitoring under paragraph 3 shall be provided by deployers to the provider.' For the bank as deployer sharing monitoring data with the vendor-provider: (1) Data that must be shared — data relevant to the post-market monitoring plan (Art.72(3)) which the provider uses to assess ongoing AI performance; for credit scoring AI, this includes: model input data characteristics (distribution of applicant profiles over time); model output distributions (score distributions over time); credit decision outcomes over time (performance of AI-approved vs AI-rejected applicants, where available); customer complaint and appeal data related to AI decisions; human override rates and patterns; any serious incidents related to the AI; (2) Privacy and data protection — the data-sharing arrangement must comply with GDPR; personal data shared with the vendor requires: legal basis (typically contract performance or legitimate interests); data processing agreement with the vendor; appropriate security measures; data minimization (aggregate statistics where possible rather than individual applicant data); (3) Contractual structure — the SLA or vendor contract should include: specific data points to be reported; reporting frequency (monthly aggregate statistics; immediate notification of serious incidents); data format and transmission security specifications; vendor's obligations to maintain data confidentiality; (4) Aggregate vs. individual data — prefer aggregate statistical data over individual applicant data to minimize privacy risk while satisfying monitoring needs; (5) AI Review Committee obligation — include Art.72(4) data-sharing requirements in vendor contract template; verify data-sharing arrangements are GDPR-compliant before execution.
notes: Art.72(4) 배포자의 모니터링 데이터 공유; GDPR 병행 고려

question: The bank's marketing AI creates comprehensive 'financial wellness scores' combining credit data, social media activity, purchase patterns, and behavioral data to predict customer product needs. The AI Review Committee must assess whether this approaches the Article 5(1)(c) social scoring prohibition.
ground_truth: Article 5(1)(c) prohibits: 'AI systems evaluating or classifying natural persons or groups of persons over a period of time based on their social behaviour or known or predicted personal or professional characteristics, with the social score leading to either or both of the following: (i) detrimental or unfavourable treatment of certain natural persons or groups of persons in social contexts that are unrelated to the contexts in which the data was originally generated or collected; (ii) detrimental or unfavourable treatment of certain natural persons or groups of persons that is unjustified or disproportionate to their social behaviour or its gravity.' For the comprehensive financial wellness score: (1) 'Social behaviour' component — incorporating social media activity, purchase patterns, and behavioral data goes beyond financial creditworthiness data; these elements reflect 'social behaviour' and 'personal characteristics'; this is a significant red flag for social scoring proximity; (2) 'Over a period of time' — longitudinal scoring based on behavioral patterns over time is a characteristic of social scoring systems; (3) 'Detrimental treatment in unrelated contexts' — if the financial wellness score influences credit decisions based on social media behavior or lifestyle choices unrelated to actual credit risk, this creates treatment in financial contexts based on data generated in social contexts; this satisfies Art.5(1)(c)(i); (4) 'Unjustified or disproportionate' — using social media data to determine financial product offers without demonstrated actuarial connection to financial risk may be disproportionate; (5) Private sector question — Art.5(1)(c) explicitly states this prohibition applies to AI systems operated by public authorities; a private bank's comprehensive scoring system may not fall within the prohibition's literal scope (targeting public authority social scoring); however, the financial regulator and AI supervisory authority may view comprehensive private sector behavioral scoring as approaching the prohibited practice; (6) Strong caution — the AI Review Committee should require rigorous legal analysis before approving any AI system combining social behavioral data with financial decision-making.
notes: Art.5(1)(c) 사회적 점수화 금지; 민간 금융 AI와의 근접성

question: The Korean bank's AI team in Seoul develops a credit scoring AI deployed for its EU customers. Article 22 requires providers established outside the EU to designate an authorised representative. What must this representative do?
ground_truth: Article 22 requires that providers of High-Risk AI systems established in third countries (including Korea) must, prior to making their High-Risk AI systems available on the EU market, designate in writing an authorised representative established in the Union. Obligations and structure: (1) Designation requirement — the authorised representative must be established in the Union (EU Member State); a subsidiary, European legal entity, or contracted EU-based firm can serve; (2) Authorised representative mandate (Art.22(2)) — the representative must be authorised to perform on behalf of the provider: compliance with the obligations of the provider under the EU AI Act; cooperation with national competent authorities; registration in the EU database on behalf of the provider; actions related to market surveillance; (3) Mandate scope (Art.22(3)) — the written mandate must specify: the AI systems covered; the representative's authority to act on behalf of the provider; contact information for competent authority interactions; (4) Compliance responsibilities — the authorised representative can be held jointly liable with the provider for non-compliance; this creates a direct EU regulatory interface; (5) Practical options for the Korean bank: EU banking subsidiary — the bank's existing EU banking subsidiary can be designated as authorised representative; EU law firm — law firms with EU AI Act expertise can serve as authorised representatives; EU AI compliance specialist — dedicated third-party authorised representative services are emerging; (6) The authorised representative must have: access to the AI system's technical documentation; ability to respond promptly to competent authority requests; authority to take corrective actions on behalf of the Korean provider; (7) Registration — the authorised representative's details must be included in the EU database registration (Art.49) and in the EU Declaration of Conformity (Art.47).
notes: Art.22 EU 공인 대표; 역외 공급자 필수 요건

question: The bank discovers that sophisticated applicants are gaming the credit scoring AI by strategically arranging their financial data to achieve artificially high scores. How does Article 15 of the EU AI Act address adversarial robustness, and what must the committee do?
ground_truth: Article 15(1) requires High-Risk AI systems to achieve, in light of their intended purpose, an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity, and to be resilient with regard to errors, faults or inconsistencies that may occur within the system or the environment in which the system operates, in particular due to interactions with natural persons or other systems. For the credit scoring AI gaming scenario: (1) Adversarial robustness requirement — Art.15(1) references resilience against 'attempts by third parties to alter the use, outputs or performance of the High-Risk AI system'; deliberately gaming the AI by manipulating financial data inputs is precisely such an attempt; (2) Required response — the provider/deployer must: assess the vulnerability (how many applicants are gaming and by how much?); implement technical countermeasures: adversarial training — incorporate adversarial examples into training to improve model robustness; anomaly detection — flag unusual input patterns that may indicate gaming; velocity monitoring — detect rapid changes in financial behavior preceding loan applications; consistency checks — validate financial data across multiple sources; (3) Balance with legitimate behavior — the committee must distinguish between: fraudulent gaming — fabricating financial data (criminal); legitimate optimization — improving genuine financial behavior before applying (acceptable); borderline cases — arranging finances optimally within legitimate bounds; (4) Art.9 risk management update — add 'adversarial input gaming' as an identified risk to the Art.9 risk management system; document mitigation measures; (5) Art.10 data governance — assess whether adversarial examples should be incorporated into training data to improve robustness (Art.10 data governance applies to any training data changes); (6) Technical documentation update — document the vulnerability and mitigation approach in the Annex IV technical documentation.
notes: AI 게이밍 대응; 적대적 강건성 요건

question: Article 57 of the EU AI Act requires Member States to establish AI regulatory sandboxes. How can financial institutions use EU AI sandboxes to innovate with High-Risk AI while managing compliance risk?
ground_truth: Articles 57 and 58 establish AI regulatory sandboxes as controlled environments for developing, testing, and validating innovative AI systems before full market deployment. For financial institutions: (1) Sandbox purpose (Art.57(1)) — sandboxes provide a controlled environment where innovative AI systems can be developed and tested under competent authority supervision; High-Risk financial AI that is novel or uncertain can be tested with regulatory guidance; (2) Benefits for financial institutions: regulatory clarity — sandbox participation provides direct dialogue with competent authorities on compliance interpretations; reduced uncertainty — test innovative AI approaches before full compliance investment; regulatory goodwill — authorities view sandbox participation favorably; early warning system — identify compliance issues before commercial deployment; (3) Sandbox eligibility (Art.57(2)) — priority access for SMEs including startups (Art.62); AI systems that are innovative and present uncertainty about compliance; (4) Sandbox operation (Art.58) — participants: operate under a sandbox plan agreed with the authority; maintain data protection and fundamental rights protections; can process personal data for specific testing purposes under special conditions (Art.59); must report results and exit within agreed timeframe; (5) Liability during testing (Art.57(5)) — participation in sandbox activities does not affect competent authority supervisory and corrective powers; limited liability protection may be available in some circumstances; (6) Financial regulatory sandbox coordination — in many EU Member States, financial regulators operate their own innovation sandboxes (EBA Innovation Hub, national financial sandbox programs); the EU AI Act sandbox should be coordinated with existing financial regulatory sandboxes to avoid duplication; (7) AI Review Committee planning — identify novel financial AI projects that would benefit from sandbox participation; engage with national competent authority for sandbox application procedures.
notes: Art.57 AI 규제 샌드박스; 금융 혁신과 컴플라이언스 리스크 관리

question: The credit scoring AI was trained primarily on loan data from Western European markets. The bank now wants to deploy it in Central and Eastern European markets. Article 10(2) requires representativeness. What must the AI Review Committee require?
ground_truth: Article 10(2)(b) requires training, validation, and testing datasets to be 'sufficiently representative' for the AI's intended purpose, including 'representative in light of the intended purpose, in particular in relation to the persons or groups of persons on which the high-risk AI system is intended to be used.' For extending credit scoring AI to Central and Eastern European (CEE) markets: (1) Representativeness gap assessment — the AI Review Committee must assess whether Western European training data adequately represents CEE market conditions: different credit market structures and histories; different income distribution patterns; different regulatory and economic environments; potentially different cultural patterns of credit behavior; different levels of formal financial market participation; (2) Required validation for CEE deployment — before deploying in CEE markets: test model performance on CEE customer samples; compare AUC, calibration, and fairness metrics in CEE markets vs. Western Europe; assess whether key predictive features (income type, employment sector) have the same relationship to creditworthiness in CEE markets; (3) Art.10(2)(b) compliance options: supplement training data with CEE market data — requires GDPR-compliant data collection; local market transfer learning — adapt the model for CEE markets using local data; separate regional models — develop CEE-specific models if the performance gap is too large to bridge; (4) Geographic fairness assessment — evaluate whether the Western European model systematically over- or under-scores CEE applicants relative to their actual creditworthiness; (5) Documentation requirement — document the geographic representativeness assessment and validation results in the technical documentation; (6) Risk management update — add geographic representation risk to the Art.9 risk management system if CEE deployment proceeds with known representativeness limitations.
notes: 지역별 대표성; 중동유럽 시장 확장 시 Art.10 준수

question: The bank's credit scoring AI performance degrades significantly during a rapid economic downturn. The AI Review Committee must decide whether to suspend the AI under Article 26(4). What factors should guide this decision?
ground_truth: Article 26(4) requires deployers to inform providers and suspend use of High-Risk AI when they have reason to believe the use is not compliant with the AI Act. For performance degradation during economic downturn: (1) Non-compliance determination — the fundamental question is whether the degraded AI still meets its obligations under the AI Act; key assessment questions: does current performance meet the accuracy thresholds specified in instructions for use (Art.13)? Do fairness metrics remain within acceptable bounds? Are the fundamental rights of affected persons still adequately protected? (2) Materiality threshold — 'reason to believe' non-compliance suggests a meaningful threshold, not every performance fluctuation; minor accuracy degradation within specified tolerance bands may not trigger mandatory suspension; systematic degradation below predefined thresholds that was specified in the instructions for use likely does trigger the obligation; (3) Factors favoring suspension: AUC or Gini coefficient degraded below predefined minimum threshold; fairness metrics now showing disproportionate rejection rates for protected groups; increased serious incident rate (wrongful rejections at scale); inability to explain current AI outputs meaningfully; (4) Factors allowing continued operation with enhanced monitoring: performance within specification tolerances even if below optimal; human oversight is strengthened to catch errors; the root cause is understood and corrective action is underway; (5) Suspension process: notify vendor-provider of performance concerns; jointly assess compliance status; if suspension warranted, communicate to affected customers and notify competent authority; activate fallback scoring methodology; (6) Partial suspension option — suspend only fully automated AI decisions; continue AI-assisted decisions with mandatory human review; (7) Documentation — record the suspension decision, rationale, and remediation plan.
notes: Art.26(4) 사용 중단 결정; 경기 침체 성능 저하 대응

question: The AI Review Committee must implement a systematic process for delivering Article 86 explanations to credit applicants. What process design ensures compliance with the 'clear and meaningful' standard?
ground_truth: Article 86 grants the right to 'clear and meaningful explanations of the role the AI system played in the decision-making procedure and the main elements of the decision taken.' Process design for credit rejection explanations: (1) Explanation generation automation — for every credit rejection decision, the system should automatically generate an explanation package containing: the AI system's role (e.g., 'Our credit assessment AI evaluated your application and identified the following factors as most significant'); the main elements (top 3-5 factors most negatively influencing the credit assessment, with specific values where appropriate: 'Your debt-to-income ratio of 62% is above our guideline of 45%'); (2) Delivery channel and timing — explanations should be: available immediately upon rejection notification; accessible through the customer's online banking portal; deliverable in writing upon request; provided in the official language of the EU Member State where the application was made; (3) Clarity standard implementation — plain language testing: test explanation comprehension with representative customer samples; target 8th grade reading level; avoid technical jargon (no 'AUC,' 'feature importance,' etc.); use concrete, relatable language; (4) Meaningfulness standard — the explanation must be substantive: 'Your application was declined' is insufficient; 'Your debt-to-income ratio and recent payment history were the primary factors in our decision' begins to meet the standard; specific values and thresholds strengthen meaningfulness; (5) Multiple decision factors — if multiple factors contributed to rejection, rank them by importance and explain each; (6) Improvement guidance — where possible, indicate what improvement could strengthen a future application; (7) Competent authority accessibility — document the explanation process in technical documentation; make process description available to competent authority on request; (8) The AI Review Committee must pre-approve the explanation templates and test them with representative customer groups before deployment.
notes: Art.86 설명권 프로세스 설계; 명확하고 의미 있는 설명 기준

question: The bank's legal team uses an AI system to analyze contracts and predict litigation outcomes to inform dispute resolution decisions. Does Annex III §8 classify this as High-Risk AI?
ground_truth: Annex III §8 covers Administration of Justice and Democratic Processes. §8(a) classifies as High-Risk: 'AI systems intended to be used by a judicial authority or on their behalf to assist a judicial authority in researching and interpreting facts and the law and in applying the law to a concrete set of facts, or to be used in a similar way in alternative dispute resolution.' For the bank's legal AI: (1) 'Judicial authority or on their behalf' — the key qualifier is use by or on behalf of a judicial authority; a private bank's legal team using AI for internal contract analysis and litigation prediction is NOT a judicial authority and is NOT acting on behalf of a judicial authority; (2) §8(a) does not apply — private sector legal AI used for business decision-making (not judicial decision-making) does not qualify under §8(a); (3) Alternative dispute resolution angle — the provision includes 'alternative dispute resolution' (ADR); if the bank uses the AI to make decisions in formal ADR proceedings where it acts as the ADR body, §8(a) may be closer to applying; purely internal litigation strategy AI is not ADR; (4) Current assessment — the bank's internal legal AI for contract analysis and litigation prediction is NOT High-Risk under Annex III §8; (5) Other risk considerations — even without High-Risk classification: Art.50 transparency obligations may apply if the AI interacts with external parties; GDPR applies to any personal data processed by the legal AI; appropriate governance is warranted given the AI's influence on legal strategy; (6) AI Review Committee approach — apply lighter governance appropriate to a non-High-Risk AI with significant business impact; document the classification rationale.
notes: Annex III §8 사법 AI; 민간 법무 AI 분류

question: The EU AI Act uses terms like 'provider,' 'deployer,' 'operator,' and 'user.' A bank that develops AI internally and uses it for its own customers simultaneously occupies multiple roles. How should the AI Review Committee navigate these overlapping roles?
ground_truth: The EU AI Act's definitional framework creates important role distinctions: (1) Provider (Art.3(3)) — 'a natural or legal person, public authority, agency or other body that develops an AI system or a general-purpose AI model or that has an AI system or a general-purpose AI model developed and places it on the market or puts it into service under its own name or trademark, whether for payment or free of charge'; a bank that develops credit scoring AI and uses it for its own customers is the provider; (2) Deployer (Art.3(4)) — 'a natural or legal person, public authority, agency or other body that uses an AI system under its authority except where the AI system is used in the course of a personal non-professional activity'; a bank that uses its own internally developed AI for credit decisions is also the deployer; (3) Dual role implications — a bank that develops AND deploys its own AI system simultaneously bears: ALL provider obligations (Chapter III Section 3 — Art.16-27, QMS, conformity assessment, registration); ALL deployer obligations (Art.26 — human oversight implementation, monitoring, FRIA, worker information); (4) Practical consequence — self-developed financial AI carries a heavier compliance burden than using vendor AI (where provider obligations fall primarily on the vendor); this is a strategic consideration in AI build-vs-buy decisions; (5) User (Art.3(5)) — 'natural persons who use an AI system'; distinct from deployers; customers using the bank's AI-driven services are 'users' with rights under Art.50, Art.86; (6) Role clarity in governance — the AI Review Committee should explicitly document for each AI system: whether the bank is provider, deployer, or both; which specific obligations fall to the bank in each role; when using vendor AI, which obligations are allocated to the vendor (provider) by contract.
notes: 공급자-배포자 이중 역할; 내부 개발 AI의 중복 의무

question: The life insurance pricing AI must be assessed for adverse impacts on elderly customers per Article 9(9). What specific assessment does the EU AI Act require for this vulnerable group?
ground_truth: Article 9(9) requires providers to give consideration to whether the High-Risk AI 'is likely to have an adverse impact on persons under the age of 18 and, as appropriate, other vulnerable groups.' For life insurance pricing AI and elderly customers: (1) 'Other vulnerable groups' — elderly persons are a paradigmatic vulnerable group for life insurance pricing AI: higher claims probabilities make them more affected by pricing AI errors; cognitive changes may affect their ability to understand and contest AI-driven pricing; fixed incomes make unaffordable AI-driven premium increases particularly harmful; they are often underrepresented in digital financial transaction training data; (2) Required assessment elements: training data representation — assess whether elderly customer data is adequately represented in the training dataset; if elderly customers are underrepresented, the model may generalize poorly to this group; performance testing — test the model's accuracy disaggregated by age group, specifically examining: calibration for elderly applicants (do predicted risk scores match actual claims rates for elderly customers?); whether error rates are higher for elderly applicants; whether pricing is disproportionately or unpredictably variable for elderly applicants; (3) Fundamental rights analysis — identify whether AI-driven pricing creates barriers to life insurance access for elderly persons; barriers to essential financial protection disproportionately harm elderly individuals on fixed incomes; (4) Mitigation measures — where adverse impacts on elderly customers are identified: mandatory human underwriter review for all applicants above a specified age threshold; enhanced explanation quality requirements for elderly applicants; alternative pricing pathways not solely dependent on AI for elderly customers; (5) Documentation — document the Art.9(9) elderly customer assessment in the Art.9 risk management system and Annex IV technical documentation.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Article 9(9); Annex III §5(c)
notes: Art.9(9) 고령 고객 취약성 평가; 생명보험 AI 특화

question: Article 26(6) requires deployers of High-Risk AI to retain logs generated by the system for an appropriate period. How long must the bank retain credit scoring AI logs, and what must be retained?
ground_truth: Article 26(6) requires: 'Deployers of High-Risk AI systems shall keep the logs automatically generated by that High-Risk AI system, to the extent such logs are under their control, for a period that is appropriate in light of the intended purpose of the High-Risk AI system and any applicable legal obligations.' For credit scoring AI logs: (1) Log content requirement — the bank must retain logs that were generated per Art.12 by the AI system; these include: timestamp and identification of when the system was used; input data that led to each credit decision output; identification of human oversight personnel involved in verification; reference data against which inputs were checked; AI system version used; (2) Retention period determination — 'appropriate period in light of intended purpose and applicable legal obligations': credit decisions are legal financial contracts; customers may challenge credit decisions; statute of limitations for credit discrimination claims is typically 3-5 years in EU jurisdictions; applicable financial record-keeping regulations in each Member State; (3) Recommended minimum retention — given the above, the bank should retain credit scoring AI logs for: minimum 5 years after the credit decision; 7 years in jurisdictions with longer financial record-keeping requirements; (4) 'To the extent under their control' — if the AI system is provided by a vendor who manages logging infrastructure, the bank may not fully control log access; contractually require the vendor to retain logs for the required period and provide deployer access on request; (5) Accessibility requirement — retained logs must be accessible to: the bank's internal audit and compliance functions; competent authorities upon request (Art.74); the deployer's own serious incident investigation; (6) The AI Review Committee must establish a formal log retention policy specifying content, period, and accessibility requirements for each High-Risk AI system.
notes: Art.26(6) 배포자 로그 보존; 신용 AI 적절한 보존 기간

question: The bank has 30 AI systems in production. The AI Review Committee conducts an Annex III classification review of all systems. What methodology should be used for efficient portfolio-level High-Risk AI classification?
ground_truth: Efficient portfolio-level EU AI Act classification requires a systematic methodology: (1) Classification decision tree — apply the following sequential logic for each AI system: Step 1 — Is the AI system explicitly listed in Annex III? Check all 8 categories (§1 biometrics; §2 critical infrastructure; §3 education; §4 employment; §5 essential services including credit/insurance; §6 law enforcement; §7 migration; §8 justice/democracy); for financial institutions, §4 and §5 are the primary relevant categories; Step 2 — If listed in Annex III, does Art.6(3) exception apply (narrow procedural task, preparatory task, decision support without influencing assessment)? Most financial credit and insurance AI does NOT qualify for this exception; Step 3 — Does Art.5 prohibition apply? If so, the AI must not be deployed; (2) Financial sector High-Risk classification outcome — expect the following classifications: Credit scoring AI: High-Risk (Annex III §5(b)); Insurance pricing AI (life/health): High-Risk (Annex III §5(c)); Employment/recruitment AI: High-Risk (Annex III §4(a)); Worker performance evaluation AI: High-Risk (Annex III §4(b)); Fraud detection AI: NOT High-Risk (explicitly excluded from §5(b)); Robo-advisory AI: likely NOT High-Risk under current Annex III; AML monitoring AI: NOT High-Risk (not law enforcement); Customer service chatbot: NOT High-Risk, but Art.50 applies; (3) Documentation — for each AI system, document: Annex III category assessed; Art.6(3) exception assessed (applied/not applied with reasoning); Art.5 prohibition assessed; final High-Risk/not High-Risk determination; applicable Chapter III obligations if High-Risk; (4) Update cadence — conduct Annex III classification review annually or when AI systems are substantially modified; monitor Annex III amendments per Art.7.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Article 6; Annex III
notes: 포트폴리오 High-Risk 분류 방법론; 30개 AI 시스템 체계적 분류

question: The bank's credit scoring AI undergoes continuous improvement with monthly feature updates. How frequently must the Art.11 technical documentation be updated, and what triggers a documentation update obligation?
ground_truth: Article 11(1) requires that technical documentation 'is drawn up before the High-Risk AI system is placed on the market or put into service and kept up to date.' Article 18 requires documentation keeping for 10 years. Together, these create a continuous documentation maintenance obligation: (1) 'Kept up to date' requirement — technical documentation is a living document that must reflect the current state of the AI system; 'kept up to date' implies that any material change to the AI system triggers a documentation update obligation; (2) What triggers mandatory documentation updates: any change to the model architecture or training methodology; any change to training or validation datasets; any change to accuracy metrics or performance benchmarks; any new risks identified through post-market monitoring; any serious incident and the corrective action taken; any change in intended purpose or deployment context; changes to the QMS or conformity assessment; (3) Monthly feature updates — minor feature updates that do not change core model behavior, training data, or performance metrics may not require full documentation updates; the bank should assess each update: is it a 'substantial modification' (Art.3(23))? If yes — new conformity assessment required; if no — documentation update to change log section (Annex IV §6) required at minimum; (4) Documentation version control — maintain version history of technical documentation; each version should be dated and describe changes from prior version; (5) Periodic comprehensive review — even without specific triggering events, conduct a comprehensive technical documentation review at least annually to ensure currency; (6) Documentation update log — maintain a change log integrated with the Annex IV §6 changes section; each entry should reference the change, date, and assessment of whether it constitutes a substantial modification; (7) The AI Review Committee should receive notification of documentation updates and annual confirmation of documentation currency.
notes: Art.11 기술 문서 최신화; 월간 업데이트 대응

question: Article 96 of the EU AI Act empowers the European Commission to issue implementation guidelines. What types of guidelines should the AI Review Committee monitor, and how should these be incorporated into governance?
ground_truth: Article 96 provides that the Commission shall issue guidelines on the practical implementation of the EU AI Act, including: (1) Guidance on Art.6(3) — how to determine whether an Annex III AI system poses a significant risk warranting High-Risk classification or can be classified as not High-Risk; financial AI guidelines may clarify borderline cases like investment advisory AI, comprehensive customer scoring; (2) Guidance on practical implementation of obligations — how to satisfy specific requirements such as: what constitutes adequate Art.9 risk management; what documentation meets Art.11 technical documentation requirements; how to conduct Art.27 fundamental rights impact assessment; what Art.86 'clear and meaningful' explanation means in practice; (3) Guidance on Art.6(3) exceptions — criteria for applying the narrow procedural task exception; (4) Guidelines for small operators — simplification guidance for SMEs (Art.96(2)(b)); (5) Monitoring strategy for AI Review Committee: designate a regulatory intelligence function monitoring Commission Official Journal for published guidelines; subscribe to EU AI Office communications; monitor European AI Board website for guidance documents; use AI Act compliance tracking services; (6) Incorporation into governance: when guidelines are published, assess current practices against guideline requirements; implement changes to bring governance into alignment; document the gap assessment and remediation timeline; (7) Guidelines as compliance safe harbor — while guidelines are not legally binding, compliance with Commission guidelines will be viewed favorably by national competent authorities and creates a presumption of compliance; (8) Financial sector coordination — monitor EBA, EIOPA, and ESMA joint guidance on EU AI Act implementation in financial services; these authorities may provide sector-specific implementation guidance that supplements general Commission guidelines.
notes: Art.96 집행위원회 지침; 금융 AI 실무 이행 가이드

question: The AI Review Committee wants to develop best practice operational procedures that go beyond minimum EU AI Act deployer obligations to demonstrate governance leadership. What additional measures should be implemented?
ground_truth: Going beyond minimum EU AI Act deployer obligations demonstrates governance leadership and reduces regulatory risk. The minimum deployer obligations under Art.26 are: appropriate use, human oversight, input data relevance, monitoring, serious risk notification, log retention, worker information, and FRIA. Best practice enhancements: (1) Enhanced human oversight beyond Art.14 minimum — implement 'human-in-the-loop' not just 'human-on-the-loop'; require human review of all adverse credit decisions, not just monitoring at aggregate level; track and analyze override rates and quality; conduct quarterly human oversight effectiveness assessments; (2) Proactive customer communication — notify customers proactively when AI-driven decisions are made (beyond mandatory Art.50 disclosure); provide unsolicited explanations of adverse decisions (beyond reactive Art.86 right); offer pre-application AI literacy information to customers; (3) External independent validation — commission annual external validation of High-Risk AI performance, fairness, and compliance; publish summary validation reports; (4) Fairness targets beyond legal minimums — set internal fairness targets more stringent than non-discrimination legal minimums; monitor against these targets quarterly; (5) Enhanced vulnerability protections — automatically route applications from identified vulnerable customers for enhanced human review, regardless of AI confidence level; (6) Supplier governance — extend governance standards to AI suppliers; conduct annual supplier compliance assessments; include EU AI Act compliance warranties in supplier contracts; (7) Transparency reporting — publish an annual AI governance report covering portfolio of AI systems, compliance status, fairness metrics, and incident statistics; (8) Continuous improvement commitment — establish a formal AI governance improvement program with annual targets; report progress to the Board and publish externally.
notes: 배포자 최소 의무 초과 모범 실무; 거버넌스 리더십

question: The bank uses AI to manage its core banking system infrastructure, monitoring network performance and automatically routing transactions. Does Annex III §2 classify this as High-Risk AI?
ground_truth: Annex III §2 covers 'AI systems intended to be used as safety components in the management and operation of critical digital infrastructure, road traffic, or in the supply of water, gas, heating or electricity.' For the bank's infrastructure management AI: (1) 'Critical digital infrastructure' — financial sector infrastructure is specifically identified as critical infrastructure in EU legislation (NIS2 Directive, DORA); banks managing critical digital infrastructure may trigger Annex III §2; (2) 'Safety components' qualifier — the key qualifier is whether the AI is used as a 'safety component'; safety components are those whose failure could lead to immediate safety risks; purely performance optimization AI (routing transactions efficiently) may not be a 'safety component'; AI that controls whether the banking system stays online or fails may qualify; (3) Scope interpretation — Annex III §2 appears primarily targeted at AI controlling public utility infrastructure (power grids, water systems); banks' internal IT infrastructure management AI may be at the margin of this category; (4) DORA intersection — banks already subject to DORA's ICT risk management requirements for critical IT systems; Annex III §2 High-Risk classification would add EU AI Act Chapter III obligations on top of DORA requirements; (5) Current assessment — the classification is not clear-cut; the bank should: assess whether its infrastructure AI functions as a 'safety component'; consider seeking competent authority guidance if uncertain; apply conservative classification (treat as High-Risk) if the AI could cause banking system failures affecting critical financial services; (6) Practical approach — apply robust IT governance and DORA compliance regardless; determine whether additional EU AI Act High-Risk obligations are needed based on the 'safety component' analysis.
notes: Annex III §2 핵심 인프라 AI; 금융 IT 인프라와의 관계

question: Article 13(1) requires High-Risk AI systems to be designed to enable deployers to use the AI appropriately. How does this requirement affect how vendors must present AI instructions to the bank's loan officers?
ground_truth: Article 13(1) states: 'High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way as to ensure that their operation is sufficiently transparent to allow deployers to interpret the system's output and use it appropriately.' For instructions delivered to bank loan officers: (1) Sufficiency of transparency — the instructions must enable the bank's loan officers (not just AI specialists) to interpret credit scoring AI outputs and use them appropriately; (2) Language and format requirements: written in plain language comprehensible to the intended deployer audience (loan officers, not data scientists); clearly explaining what the AI output means (what does a score of 720 mean? What does an amber flag mean?); describing the AI's known limitations in terms that inform operational decisions; explaining specifically when to apply human judgment and override the AI; (3) Content required by Art.13(3) specifically for loan officers: what factors most commonly influence the credit score (without revealing proprietary weights); circumstances where the AI may be unreliable (unusual income types, thin credit files, applicants from underrepresented populations); what score range and profile characteristics typically indicate the AI's high vs. low confidence; how to interpret and act on anomaly flags; (4) Training materials — instructions for use should include or be accompanied by training materials enabling loan officers to achieve and maintain the competency required by Art.14; (5) Update obligations — when the AI system changes materially, updated instructions for use must be provided; (6) AI Review Committee obligation — require vendors to provide Art.13 instructions before operational deployment; review instructions for adequacy against the needs of the bank's operational staff; provide feedback to vendors where instructions are insufficient.
